title: More Advanced Accounting
description: Once you know the three statements cold, these topics come up in more technical interviews. Each one is explained from scratch — no prior accounting course needed.

sections.title: Deferred Taxes — the gap between what you report and what you pay
sections.body:
Here's something weird: companies often report different numbers to investors than they report to the IRS. Both are legal. They just follow different rules.

When those numbers differ, a timing gap opens up. That gap is what deferred taxes are.

Example: Imagine your company buys a $10,000 machine. For accounting purposes (what you show investors), you depreciate it evenly over 5 years — $2,000/year. But for tax purposes, the IRS lets you write off more upfront — say $5,000 in year 1. This means you pay less tax now. But eventually, you'll owe more later.

That future obligation is a Deferred Tax Liability (DTL): you've saved on taxes today, but you'll pay them tomorrow.

The flip side is a Deferred Tax Asset (DTA): when you've overpaid taxes relative to your reported income — for example, if you had a big loss one year and can use it to reduce future taxes. That's a future tax savings — an asset.

On the cash flow statement: when a DTL increases, you paid less tax than your income statement implies, so it's a source of cash in CFO. When a DTA increases, you've effectively prepaid taxes, so it's a use of cash in CFO.

You don't need to calculate these precisely yet. The key is to understand why they exist and which direction they move cash.
keyPoints: GAAP rules and tax rules differ — deferred taxes bridge the gap
DTL: paid less tax now, will owe more later (e.g. accelerated depreciation)
DTA: paid more tax now, will save in future (e.g. losses to carry forward)
Increase in DTL = source of cash in CFO (like AP going up)
Increase in DTA = use of cash in CFO (like AR going up)

sections.title: Stock-Based Compensation — paying employees in equity
sections.body:
Many companies, especially tech startups, don't pay all their employees in cash. They give stock or stock options instead. This is called stock-based compensation (SBC).

From the employee's perspective, it's valuable — if the company grows, those shares are worth more. From the company's perspective, it's a cost — employees are working in exchange for something valuable.

But here's the key: no cash leaves the company. The company just issues shares.

So how does it show up in the financial statements?

Income statement: SBC is an expense, just like salary. It reduces EBIT and net income. (This is why some profitable-looking companies actually look less profitable once you account for SBC.)

Cash flow statement: Since it's non-cash, it gets added back in CFO — similar to depreciation. No cash actually left the company.

Balance sheet: Retained earnings fall (from the income statement expense). But APIC (additional paid-in capital) rises when the shares are issued. The two roughly offset, so equity stays about the same.

Why does this matter? Big tech companies have enormous SBC. When you see 'adjusted net income' or 'adjusted EBITDA,' they often exclude SBC. Be careful — that's ignoring a real cost. SBC dilutes existing shareholders even if it doesn't reduce cash.
keyPoints: SBC: employees receive equity instead of cash — a real cost even though non-cash
On IS: expense that reduces EBIT and net income
On CFS: added back in CFO (non-cash, like D&A)
On BS: retained earnings fall, APIC rises — equity roughly unchanged
SBC dilutes shareholders over time — adjusted metrics that exclude it can be misleading

sections.title: FIFO vs. LIFO — how you value what you sell matters
sections.body:
When a company sells a product, it needs to decide: which items did we 'sell' first? This matters because if prices are changing, the answer affects how much profit you report.

Think about a sneaker store that buys two batches of the same shoe:
• Batch 1 (older): 50 pairs at $60 each (bought 3 months ago)
• Batch 2 (newer): 50 pairs at $80 each (bought last month)

You then sell 50 pairs at $120 each.

FIFO (First In, First Out): assume you sold the older batch first.
• COGS: 50 × $60 = $3,000
• Revenue: 50 × $120 = $6,000
• Gross Profit: $3,000

LIFO (Last In, First Out): assume you sold the newer batch first.
• COGS: 50 × $80 = $4,000
• Revenue: 50 × $120 = $6,000
• Gross Profit: $2,000

Same revenue, same physical goods — but LIFO shows lower profit because it uses higher costs.

In a world of rising prices (inflation), LIFO means higher COGS → lower profit → lower taxes. That's why US companies sometimes prefer LIFO for tax purposes. But LIFO is banned under IFRS (the international accounting standard), which is a key difference between US and non-US companies.

When you compare companies, you need to check which method they use. To convert a LIFO company to FIFO for comparison, you add the 'LIFO reserve' disclosed in their financial notes.
keyPoints: FIFO (rising prices): older, cheaper costs → lower COGS, higher profit, more tax
LIFO (rising prices): newer, higher costs → higher COGS, lower profit, less tax
Same goods, different reported profit — inventory method is an accounting choice
LIFO is allowed under US GAAP but banned under IFRS
The LIFO reserve lets you convert between methods for comparison

sections.title: Leases — the debt that used to be hidden
sections.body:
Companies lease all kinds of things — office space, equipment, vehicles. For a long time, many of these leases were kept entirely off the balance sheet. That meant companies looked less indebted than they actually were.

Regulators eventually changed the rules. Under the current rules (ASC 842 in the US, IFRS 16 globally), most leases must now appear on the balance sheet.

Here's what that looks like:
• The company records a Right-of-Use (ROU) asset: 'we have the right to use this space.'
• And a lease liability: 'we owe future payments for this space.'

There are two types of leases:

Operating Lease (most leases — office space, equipment rentals):
• Balance sheet: ROU asset and lease liability (new under current rules)
• Income statement: one lease expense line in operating expenses
• Cash flow: the payment goes in CFO

Finance Lease (like owning — typically high-value equipment you'll use most of its life):
• Balance sheet: same ROU asset and lease liability
• Income statement: split into depreciation (above EBITDA) and interest (below EBITDA)
• Cash flow: principal repayment goes in CFF; interest in CFO

The key interview point: the lease type affects EBITDA. For an operating lease, the lease expense sits above EBITDA. For a finance lease, only depreciation sits above EBITDA, while interest is below. This can make the same company look more or less profitable depending on how leases are classified.
keyPoints: Old rules: operating leases were off-balance-sheet. New rules (ASC 842): most leases are on
Both types add ROU asset + lease liability to the balance sheet
Operating lease: single expense above EBITDA, in CFO
Finance lease: depreciation above EBITDA + interest below, principal in CFF
Lease classification affects EBITDA — matters for company comparisons

sections.title: Goodwill — what you paid for that you can't touch
sections.body:
When one company buys another, it almost always pays more than the 'book value' of the target's assets. Why? Because a company is worth more than just its stuff. It has a brand, customer relationships, talented employees, patents — things that aren't fully captured on the balance sheet.

The premium is split into two pieces:
1. Fair value adjustments to identifiable assets and liabilities (e.g. the target's equipment might be worth more than its book value)
2. Whatever's left over after those adjustments = Goodwill

Goodwill shows up as an asset on the acquirer's balance sheet. It represents 'we paid extra because we believed in this business.'

How goodwill behaves:
• It's not amortized under US GAAP — it just sits on the balance sheet
• Each year, the company tests whether it's still worth what they paid ('impairment test')
• If the business underperforms, they write down goodwill — that's a non-cash charge that hits the income statement
• No cash effect from goodwill impairment: the charge reduces income and reduces the goodwill asset, but no cash moves

Why it matters: M&A deals generate a lot of goodwill. After a big acquisition, a company's balance sheet can balloon with goodwill. If those acquisitions don't pan out, you'll see big impairment charges — which can be alarming on the income statement but don't affect cash flow.
keyPoints: Goodwill = purchase price minus fair value of net assets acquired
It represents intangible value: brand, customers, talent, know-how
Not amortized under US GAAP — stays on BS until impaired
Impairment: non-cash charge on IS, reduces goodwill on BS, no cash flow impact
Large goodwill balances signal big premium M&A — a red flag if deals don't deliver

sections.title: Depreciation — spreading the cost of assets over time
sections.body:
When you buy a piece of equipment, you don't expense the full cost on day one. You spread it out over the asset's useful life. That spreading is called depreciation.

Why not expense it immediately? Because the asset generates value over multiple years. Matching the cost to the revenue it generates over time is more accurate. (This is the matching principle in accounting.)

The most common method is straight-line:
If a $10,000 machine lasts 10 years with zero salvage value, you depreciate $1,000/year.

There's also accelerated depreciation (like Double Declining Balance): you write off more in early years, less later. This lowers your taxable income in the early years, which is why it's popular for tax purposes.

Amortization is the same idea but for intangible assets (like patents, customer lists, software). Depreciation is for physical assets (PP&E); amortization is for intangibles. Together they're called D&A.

Both are non-cash — they reduce income but no cash leaves. So in the cash flow statement, you add them back to net income in CFO.

PP&E Roll-Forward (how to track PP&E across periods):
Ending PP&E = Beginning PP&E + CapEx − Depreciation − Asset Disposals

This formula is foundational for financial modeling. If you know three of the four variables, you can solve for the fourth.
keyPoints: Depreciation spreads a physical asset's cost over its useful life
Straight-line: equal amount every year. Accelerated: more in early years
Amortization: same concept for intangible assets (patents, software, customer lists)
D&A is non-cash — add back in CFO, reduces income on IS, lowers PP&E on BS
PP&E roll-forward: beginning + CapEx − D&A − disposals = ending

sections.title: Key Financial Ratios — how to read a company's health
sections.body:
Raw numbers in financial statements are hard to compare across companies of different sizes. Ratios normalize everything so you can make apple-to-apple comparisons.

Profitability (is the company good at making money?):
• Gross Margin = Gross Profit / Revenue → what % you keep after making the product
• EBITDA Margin = EBITDA / Revenue → operating efficiency, ignoring capital structure
• Net Margin = Net Income / Revenue → what % you keep after everything

Liquidity (can the company pay its bills short-term?):
• Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities → above 1 means you can cover short-term obligations
• Quick Ratio = (Current Assets − Inventory) / Current Liabilities → stricter; excludes inventory

Leverage (how much debt does the company carry?):
• Debt / EBITDA → how many years of cash flow it would take to pay off all debt. Under 3× is generally healthy for most industries.
• Net Debt / EBITDA → same but subtracts cash from debt (because cash could offset debt)
• Interest Coverage = EBITDA / Interest Expense → can the company afford its interest payments? Above 3× is generally comfortable.

Return metrics:
• ROE = Net Income / Shareholders' Equity → how efficiently equity is generating profit
• ROA = Net Income / Total Assets → how efficiently assets are generating profit

You don't need to memorize these formulas. Understand what each is measuring, and you'll be able to derive them when you need them.
keyPoints: Gross margin, EBITDA margin, net margin measure profitability at different stages
Current ratio and quick ratio measure ability to pay short-term obligations
Debt/EBITDA measures leverage — how many years of cash flow to pay off debt
Interest coverage (EBITDA / interest) shows if the company can service its debt
ROE and ROA measure how efficiently equity and assets generate profit
